sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: Major US indices closed a volatile week higher as cautious optimism over a possible US-Iran agreement, falling oil prices and continued broadening beyond mega-cap technology offset mixed inflation data and choppy AI-related names. Small-caps led, with the Russell 2000 rising roughly 3.9%, while the Dow, S&P 500 and Nasdaq each added more than 0.65%. Size and style leadership tilted away from crowded growth as the de-escalation narrative favoured cyclicals and rate-sensitive segments. Headline CPI topped 4%, though the muted reaction reflected a view that energy is the dominant driver, and investors largely looked through it towards the following week's FOMC. Valuations in large-cap tech stayed in focus amid AI volatility, but easing geopolitical risk and lower crude underpinned the rotation and supported a constructive, if headline-driven, tone.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European equities advanced strongly, propelled by rising hopes of Middle East de-escalation and the accompanying retreat in energy prices. Germany's DAX surged around 3.92%, the UK's FTSE 100 climbed about 2.66%, France's CAC 40 rose roughly 2.05% and Italy's FTSE MIB added some 0.80%, underscoring marked regional dispersion. The ECB lifted its key rates on Thursday, its first hike since September 2023, raising the deposit facility rate to 2.25% to stop the energy shock embedding in broader inflation, with President Lagarde keeping the door open to further moves as end-2026 inflation expectations edged up to 3.0%. The European Commission trimmed its eurozone growth forecast to 0.9% for 2026, citing a major energy shock. Cyclical and rate-sensitive sectors led the rally, though the tightening backdrop tempered the upside.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: APAC markets were more mixed than their Western peers. Japanese equities edged higher, with the Nikkei 225 up around 0.39% even as the broader TOPIX slipped about 0.20%, as investors weighed a fragile US-Iran ceasefire against elevated energy prices and firm inflation. BoJ Governor Ueda's remarks were read as raising the odds of a June rate hike, signalling that countering inflation should take priority, while the 10-year JGB yield held near 2.66%. Chinese and Hong Kong shares lagged the global relief rally, weighed earlier in the week by elevated oil and softer risk appetite, with limited fresh policy support failing to provide a decisive floor. Across Asia ex-Japan, sentiment remained hostage to the energy and geopolitical backdrop, though falling crude into the weekend offered tentative support to trade-sensitive markets.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The dollar softened modestly over the week even as the broader case for a hawkish Fed, built on firm labour data and rising inflation pressure, kept it fundamentally supported. Tentative US-Iran deal hopes sparked risk-on swings that left major pairs largely range-bound: the EUR and GBP struggled to convincingly recapture 1.16 and 1.34 respectively, despite the ECB's first hike since 2023 lifting the deposit rate to 2.25% and Lagarde signalling further tightening. Sterling lacked momentum ahead of a UK by-election wildcard, while the euro's gains were capped by weak growth forecasts. The yen firmed on hawkish BoJ commentary that lifted June hike expectations. With CPI above 4% drawing little reaction, energy being the chief culprit, FX stayed cautious as traders positioned for the following week's Fed decision and a cluster of central bank meetings.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: Commodities extended their decline as the geopolitical risk premium unwound. The Bloomberg Commodity Index headed for a fourth consecutive weekly loss, down around 2.3% and trimming its year-to-date gain to roughly 20%, with 20 of 26 major futures markets lower. The reversal was triggered by President Trump cancelling planned strikes on Iran and flagging a possible weekend deal, prompting a sharp cross-asset move: crude oil and refined fuels tumbled, while gold and silver each shed some 4-5% as safe-haven demand faded and real-yield dynamics turned less supportive. Investors stayed wary, however, having weathered repeated false dawns on de-escalation. Agriculture showed signs of stabilising, with gains in wheat, coffee and cattle offsetting continued weakness in soybeans, corn, sugar and cotton. The slide in energy reinforced the disinflationary, risk-on tone rippling across other assets.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: Government bonds rallied as easing Middle East tensions and falling energy prices pulled yields lower across the curve. The 10-year UST yield slipped to around 4.48% from 4.53% the prior week, the 2-year fell roughly 7bp to about 4.08% and the 30-year eased to near 4.97%, a modest bull-flattening. The move held despite headline CPI topping 4%, as markets judged energy the principal driver and looked ahead to the following week's FOMC, where the Fed was widely expected to hold at 3.50%-3.75% amid speculation the dot plot could turn more hawkish and strip out previously projected 2026 cuts. With about half of policymakers seen likely to pencil in at least one hike, the front end stayed anchored even as the long end benefited from geopolitical relief. Credit tone remained firm, with IG resilient and HY supported by the broader risk-on mood.
